_comment: Curated per-investor metadata shown on /investors/<slug>/ pages. Schema: name → { people: [[person, role], …], about: 'one-line history' }. Names must match investor names in the dataset exactly; build-pages.mjs warns on mismatches. People only included where publicly verifiable — extend rather than guess.

ether.fi Ventures.about: Venture arm of the ether.fi liquid-restaking protocol (founded 2023 by Mike Silagadze) — backs teams building the onchain banking stack it pioneered with its own Cash card, starting with a co-lead of Hyperbeat's 2025 seed.

Electric Capital.about: Palo Alto crypto-native fund founded 2018, best known for its annual Crypto Developer Report — the industry's census of open-source builders — alongside early bets across DeFi and onchain finance.

Coinbase Ventures.about: Corporate venture arm of Coinbase (est. 2018); one of the most active crypto investors ever with 400+ portfolio companies, seeding the onchain economy its exchange settles.

Stellar Development Foundation.about: Non-profit stewarding the Stellar network since 2014; its enterprise fund backs payments and remittance apps that settle on Stellar rails — including COCA's self-custodial card.

Ribbit Capital.about: Palo Alto fintech-only VC founded by Micky Malka in 2012 — the purest financial-services thesis in venture, with early positions in Robinhood, Revolut, Nubank and Coinbase.

Tiger Global.about: New York crossover fund founded in 2001 by Chase Coleman, a 'Tiger cub' of Julian Robertson's Tiger Management; famous for its hyper-fast growth-stage deployment of 2020–21.

SoftBank.about: Masayoshi Son's Tokyo conglomerate whose $100B Vision Fund (2017) was the largest tech fund ever raised — mega-cheques into SoFi, Klarna, OPay and Paytm.

Tencent.about: Shenzhen internet giant behind WeChat; one of the most prolific strategic investors in global fintech, with quiet minority stakes in neobanks on four continents.

Y Combinator.about: The Silicon Valley accelerator, founded 2005 by Paul Graham and Jessica Livingston; its batches produced Stripe, Coinbase and Brex — and a steady stream of emerging-market neobanks.

Valar Ventures.about: New York fintech fund co-founded in 2010 by Peter Thiel with Andrew McCormack and James Fitzgerald; the closest thing venture has to a dedicated neobank fund (N26, Wise, Qonto).

DST Global.about: Yuri Milner's late-stage fund, founded 2009 after his landmark early Facebook bet; a fixture of the biggest fintech growth rounds (Nubank, Revolut, Chime, Brex).

Sequoia Capital.about: The Sand Hill Road institution founded by Don Valentine in 1972 — Apple to Stripe; now led by former PayPal CFO Roelof Botha.

Andreessen Horowitz.about: Founded 2009 by Marc Andreessen and Ben Horowitz; ~$45B under management with dedicated fintech and crypto practices — the widest wave-spread of any firm in this dataset.

Peak XV Partners.about: Formerly Sequoia Capital India & Southeast Asia, spun off independent in 2023; the region's largest venture franchise and a kingmaker in Indian fintech.

Ant Group.about: Alibaba's fintech affiliate and operator of Alipay; its stakes in bKash, GCash, Paytm, DANA and TrueMoney form a super-app alliance across South and Southeast Asia.

General Catalyst.about: Founded 2000 in Cambridge, MA and reshaped by Hemant Taneja into a global 'resilience' investment company; backer of Stripe, Ramp and Monzo.

Lightspeed.about: Menlo Park multi-stage firm founded in 2000, now global across the US, India, Israel and Southeast Asia with a large fintech practice.

Accel.about: Palo Alto firm founded 1983, famous for the early Facebook round; its London office built one of Europe's strongest fintech portfolios (Monzo, Lydia, Trade Republic).

Founders Fund.about: Peter Thiel's San Francisco fund, founded 2005 with PayPal alumni; contrarian, concentrated bets — Ramp, Wave and Plasma among its money-app positions.

General Atlantic.about: New York growth-equity pioneer founded in 1980, originally to invest Duty Free Shoppers co-founder Chuck Feeney's fortune; global fintech growth rounds from Chime to PhonePe.

QED Investors.about: Alexandria, VA fintech specialist founded 2007 by Capital One co-founder Nigel Morris and Frank Rotman; arguably the deepest consumer-credit expertise in venture.

Coatue.about: The Laffont brothers' New York crossover fund, founded 1999 as a 'Tiger cub' hedge fund and now a major private tech growth investor.

GIC.about: Singapore's sovereign wealth fund, established 1981 to manage the country's foreign reserves; a patient anchor in late-stage fintech rounds worldwide.

Index Ventures.about: Founded in Geneva in 1996, now London/San Francisco; one of Europe's defining venture firms and an early Revolut, Wise and Adyen backer.

Insight Partners.about: New York software-growth investor founded 1995, known for massive ScaleUp cheques; led N26's landmark Series D.

Kaszek.about: Latin America's largest VC, founded 2011 by MercadoLibre co-founder Hernan Kazah and its former CFO Nicolas Szekasy; first institutional money into Nubank.

Allianz X.about: The digital-investments arm of Munich insurance giant Allianz, writing strategic growth cheques into fintech and insurtech (N26, Wealthsimple).

Augmentum Fintech.about: London Stock Exchange-listed fintech investment trust (LSE: AUGM) — permanent capital dedicated to European fintech, from Tide to Zopa.

Goldman Sachs.about: The Wall Street bank's principal-investing and growth-equity arms have seeded neobanks worldwide even while running its own consumer experiment, Marcus.

Greenoaks.about: San Francisco firm founded by Neil Mehta known for concentrated, long-duration growth positions — Brex among its best-known fintech bets.

Partech.about: Paris-headquartered global fund whose Partech Africa vehicle is one of the continent's largest — early money into Wave and Djamo.

PayPal Ventures.about: The corporate venture arm of PayPal, investing off the payments giant's balance sheet into fintech adjacencies across Europe, Asia and LatAm.

Qatar Investment Authority.about: Qatar's sovereign wealth fund, established 2005; late-stage anchor cheques including Starling Bank's largest round.

Speedinvest.about: Vienna-based pre-seed and seed firm founded 2011 by Oliver Holle; one of Europe's most active early-stage fintech investors.

Stripe.about: The Collison brothers' payments company invests strategically off its own balance sheet — Step, Ramp and Wave all took Stripe money.

Temasek.about: Singapore state investment company founded 1974; a heavyweight in growth fintech across India and Southeast Asia.

Union Square Ventures.about: New York thesis-driven firm founded 2003 by Fred Wilson and Brad Burnham; early Twitter, Etsy and Coinbase.

Warburg Pincus.about: New York private-equity firm with roots to 1939 and more than $80B under management; growth positions in Varo, GCash and MoMo.

BBVA.about: The Spanish banking group was the earliest big-bank neobank investor — a major Atom Bank shareholder and backer of Brazil's Neon (it also owned Simple, which it later shut).

Crosslink Capital.about: San Francisco crossover firm founded 1989; wrote some of the earliest checks into US challenger banking, including Chime's first round.

Drive Capital.about: Columbus, Ohio firm founded 2013 by ex-Sequoia partners to back startups outside Silicon Valley — Greenlight and KOHO among its fintech wins.

Elevation Capital.about: Formerly SAIF Partners India; one of the country's oldest venture franchises and the earliest institutional backer of Paytm.

Emtek.about: Indonesian media-and-tech conglomerate (SCTV, Vidio); strategic investor in DANA and Grab's local ecosystem, co-anchor of Superbank.

Grab.about: Southeast Asia's ride-hailing super-app; its financial-services ambitions run through stakes in OVO and the Superbank joint venture with Singtel and Emtek.

Greyhound Capital.about: London growth-equity firm focused on fintech and software scale-ups across Europe — backer of Lunar and albo.

Greylock.about: One of the oldest venture firms in America (founded 1965); consumer and enterprise franchise that backed Wealthsimple and Xapo.

HongShan.about: Sequoia Capital China until the 2023 three-way split; Neil Shen's Shanghai/Hong Kong powerhouse now invests globally under the HongShan name.

IFC.about: The World Bank Group's private-sector arm — development capital behind financial-inclusion champions like bKash and Maya.

Kinnevik.about: Stockholm-listed investment company controlled by the Stenbeck family since 1936; pivoted from media to growth tech, backing Monese and Lunar.

Lightrock.about: Global impact-growth investor backed by LGT, the Liechtenstein princely family's bank; portfolio spans Niyo and Moniepoint.

Menlo Ventures.about: Sand Hill Road firm founded 1976; among the earliest institutional believers in Chime and Bluevine.

Mizuho.about: One of Japan's three megabanks; writes strategic cheques into Asian digital finance, from Tonik in the Philippines to Vietnam's MoMo.

Monashees.about: Brazil's first institutional venture firm, founded 2005 in São Paulo; early money into Neon and Nomad.

Pantera Capital.about: Dan Morehead's firm launched the first US bitcoin fund in 2013 and remains a crypto-native institution — LatAm exchange-neobanks Ripio and Bitso in portfolio.

Point72 Ventures.about: The early-stage arm affiliated with Steve Cohen's Point72; fintech-heavy portfolio including Argentina's Brubank and Manila's Tonik.

Pollen Street Capital.about: London private-equity and credit firm spun out of RBS's equity-finance unit in 2013; financial-services specialist and long-time bunq backer.

Prosus Ventures.about: The venture arm of Prosus (Naspers' Amsterdam-listed international assets) — the group whose early Tencent stake became the best VC trade ever.

Quona Capital.about: Financial-inclusion fintech fund spun out of nonprofit Accion in 2015; invests across LatAm, Africa, and Asia (Klar, Global66).

Redpoint.about: Sand Hill Road firm founded 1999; multi-stage software and consumer investor with fintech positions from NorthOne to Comun.

Silver Lake.about: The world's largest technology private-equity firm, founded 1999; late-stage positions in SoFi and Klarna.

Spark Capital.about: Boston/San Francisco firm founded 2005 (Twitter, Slack, Anthropic); fintech bets include Deel and Brazil's Nomad.

Stripes.about: New York growth-equity firm founded by Ken Fox; consumer and software focus with fintech positions in Novo and Nomad.

Target Global.about: Berlin-headquartered pan-European fund; early Revolut backer whose portfolio also spans Lili and Kuda.

TCV.about: Crossover growth firm founded 1995 (Netflix's defining backer); led Revolut's 2020 round and holds Qonto and Allica.

Thrive Capital.about: Josh Kushner's New York firm, founded 2010; concentrated conviction positions from Stripe to OpenAI, with Ramp and Trade Republic in fintech.

Toscafund.about: London hedge fund and financials specialist; one of the earliest institutional shareholders in both Atom Bank and OakNorth.

Walmart.about: The US retail giant majority-owns One (with Ribbit Capital) as its embedded-finance play, and holds a large stake in India's PhonePe via Flipkart.

3one4 Capital.about: Bangalore early-stage firm founded 2016 by the Pai brothers; deep in Indian consumer fintech, including Open's SMB neobank.

83North.about: London/Tel Aviv firm spun out of Greylock's Israel/Europe practice; Laurel Bowden's fintech track record includes Bluevine and Wise-rival Zopa.

ADQ.about: Abu Dhabi's state holding company (established 2018); majority anchor of Wio Bank, the UAE's flagship digital bank.

AirTree Ventures.about: Sydney firm founded 2014, one of Australia/NZ's largest VCs; backed Kiwi freelancer-bank Hnry.

Alibaba.about: The Chinese e-commerce group; its Paytm position (alongside Ant Group's) made it one of the defining backers of Indian fintech's first wave.

Alibaba Entrepreneurs Fund.about: The Hong Kong non-profit initiative funded by Alibaba to back local founders; an early institutional supporter of WeLab Bank's parent.

Alpha Wave.about: Global fund (formerly Falcon Edge) with Abu Dhabi anchor capital; growth positions across Indian fintech including Fi Money.

Altos Ventures.about: Menlo Park firm with an unmatched Korea franchise — the defining early backer of Toss (Viva Republica), Korea's fintech decacorn.

Alven.about: Paris seed and Series A firm, one of France's oldest independent VCs; first-round money into Qonto.

Anthemis.about: London/New York fintech-only firm founded 2010; one of the first funds built entirely around reinventing financial services (early Tide, Monzo pre-history via passion for UK fintech).

Apax.about: London private-equity firm with roots in the 1970s (Alan Patricof, Ronald Cohen); its digital fund holds SMB-banking leader Tide.

Apis Partners.about: London growth-equity firm investing exclusively in financial services across growth markets; a key TymeBank backer.

Atalaya Capital.about: New York credit and special-situations manager; provides the asset-backed firepower behind lenders like Allica.

Atomico.about: Founded 2006 by Skype co-founder Niklas Zennström to back European founders; an early Klarna believer.

Avenir Growth.about: New York growth firm known for concentrated fintech conviction — Flutterwave's biggest backer and a Current investor.

B Capital.about: Global growth firm co-founded by Facebook co-founder Eduardo Saverin; strong Asia footprint including India's Fi Money.

BAI Capital.about: Growth fund born as Bertelsmann Asia Investments; backs founders building for global markets, including Mexico's Stori.

Baillie Gifford.about: Edinburgh asset manager founded 1908, famous for decade-long public/private positions (Tesla, Amazon) — and a patient Wise shareholder.

Bain Capital Ventures.about: The venture arm of Bain Capital; Matt Harris's fintech practice is among the most respected in the industry (Relay, Flywire, Justworks).

Balderton Capital.about: London firm born as Benchmark Europe in 2000; seed-stage Revolut backer and a fixture of European fintech cap tables.

Bank of America.about: The second-largest US bank; a strategic investor in Greenwood, the Atlanta neobank serving Black and Latino communities.

Battery Ventures.about: Boston firm founded 1983 investing from seed to buyout; backed SMB-banking startup NorthOne.

Benchmark.about: The famously small equal-partnership firm (eBay, Uber, Twitter); took an early position in Wences Casares' Xapo.

Berkshire Hathaway.about: Warren Buffett's conglomerate made a rare fintech exception with a $500M Nubank position — validation from the most famously tech-averse investor alive.

Better Tomorrow Ventures.about: San Francisco fintech-only seed fund founded 2019 by Sheel Mohnot and NerdWallet co-founder Jake Gibson; first cheques into Relay and dozens of fintechs.

Bezos Expeditions.about: Jeff Bezos' personal investment office; its African fintech bet Chipper Cash was one of the vehicle's highest-profile venture positions.

Bitfinex.about: The crypto exchange (iFinex group, closely tied to Tether); strategic backer of the Plasma stablecoin-chain ecosystem behind Plasma One.

BlackRock.about: The world's largest asset manager (~$10T+); took a growth stake in Brazil's Neon in 2022.

Blockchain Capital.about: One of the first dedicated crypto VCs (founded 2013 in San Francisco); backer of Kraken and its Krak money app.

BlueYard Capital.about: Berlin fund investing in decentralization and open-web theses; early backer of P2P dollar network Airtm.

Blume Ventures.about: Mumbai/Bangalore early-stage firm, one of India's largest homegrown seed funds; backed credit-card challenger slice.

Boost VC.about: Adam Draper's San Mateo accelerator, an early crypto true-believer (500+ frontier-tech startups); seeded Argentina's Ripio.

British International Investment.about: The UK government's development-finance institution (formerly CDC, founded 1948); impact capital into TymeBank's financial-inclusion mission.

Canapi Ventures.about: Fintech fund raised largely from US banks, co-founded by former Comptroller of the Currency Gene Ludwig; invests where banks and startups meet (Greenlight).

CapitalG.about: Alphabet's independent growth fund (founded 2013); backed savings app Albert among its fintech positions.

Chrysalis.about: London-listed investment trust holding late-stage private growth companies; one of Starling Bank's largest outside shareholders.

Citi Ventures.about: Citigroup's corporate venture arm, investing strategically in fintech infrastructure and SMB finance (Bluevine).

Clermont Group.about: Singapore-based conglomerate of New Zealand billionaire Richard Chandler; OakNorth's founding anchor investor.

CoinFund.about: New York crypto-native fund founded 2015, one of the earliest institutional DeFi investors; backs EtherFi's cash products.

Costanoa Ventures.about: San Francisco seed firm founded by Netscape's first product manager Greg Sands; backed immigrant-focused neobank Comun.

CP Group.about: Thailand's largest conglomerate (Charoen Pokphand, controlled by the Chearavanont family); TrueMoney sits inside its Ascend Money fintech arm.

Craft Ventures.about: David Sacks' San Francisco fund (PayPal COO, Yammer founder); SaaS-first portfolio with bitcoin-rewards app Fold in fintech.

Creaegis.about: Indian growth-equity firm investing in technology and financial services; backed cross-border neobank Zolve.

Creandum.about: Stockholm firm famous for Spotify's seed; among the first institutional backers of Berlin's Trade Republic.

CRV.about: Charles River Ventures, founded 1970 — one of America's oldest early-stage firms; wrote early Mercury cheques.

Deciens Capital.about: Fintech-only early-stage fund run by Standard Treasury founder Dan Kimerling; led Chipper Cash's seed.

Development Partners International.about: London private-equity firm investing exclusively in Africa (~$3B AUM); growth capital behind Moniepoint's rise.

DFJ Growth.about: The growth-stage arm of the Draper venture network (Tesla, SpaceX, Twitter); backed business-banking platform Rho.

Digital Currency Group.about: Barry Silbert's crypto holding company (Grayscale, CoinDesk, Genesis); prolific seed investor across 200+ crypto startups including Ripio.

Dragoneer.about: San Francisco growth fund founded by Marc Stad; public/private crossover positions including France's Sumeria (Lydia).

e&.about: The UAE telecom group formerly known as Etisalat; consortium partner in Wio Bank alongside ADQ and Mubadala.

Earlybird.about: Berlin/Munich firm founded 1997, one of Germany's venture pioneers; the earliest institutional backer of N26.

Eldridge.about: Todd Boehly's holding company (stakes from the LA Dodgers to Chelsea FC); growth investor in Canada's KOHO.

Fidelity.about: The Boston asset-management giant; its funds led Starling Bank's later rounds via Fidelity Management & Research.

Flourish Ventures.about: Fintech-for-fairness fund spun out of Omidyar Network in 2019 with $500M+ of evergreen capital; backs FairMoney and inclusion-first fintechs globally.

Foundation Capital.about: Palo Alto firm founded 1995; Charles Moldow's fintech practice (LendingClub, Rappi) took an early Current position.

Framework Ventures.about: DeFi-native crypto fund founded 2019; among the largest early stakeholders in Chainlink and Synthetix, now backing stablecoin banking (Plasma One).

Garage Capital.about: Waterloo, Ontario seed fund plugged into Canada's engineering-founder network; early Relay backer.

Gates Foundation.about: The world's largest private foundation; its financial-services-for-the-poor program made a rare direct equity commitment to Bangladesh's bKash.

Globe Telecom.about: The Philippine telecom giant; GCash grew out of Globe's mobile-money unit and remains anchored by it (with Ant Group and Ayala).

Golden Ventures.about: Toronto seed fund founded 2011; anchor of the Canadian early-stage ecosystem and Neo Financial backer.

Goodwater Capital.about: Consumer-only VC founded 2014 (Kakao, Coupang heritage); one of the most systematic consumer-fintech investors — Stori, Monzo, Toss.

Google (Africa Investment Fund).about: Google's $50M vehicle for African startups announced in 2021; Moniepoint was one of its first fintech investments.

Group 11.about: Los Angeles fintech-only fund run by Dovi Frances; early and repeated backer of freelancer-bank Lili.

Headline.about: Global multi-stage firm (formerly e.ventures) investing from six offices worldwide; backed crypto-neobank Deblock in France.

Heartcore Capital.about: Copenhagen-based consumer-tech VC (formerly Sunstone); early backer of US migrant-banking app Majority.

Hoxton Ventures.about: London seed firm known for contrarian early bets (Deliveroo, Darktrace); seeded crypto-neobank Deblock.

HRTG (Sequoia Heritage).about: Sequoia Heritage — the endowment-style wealth fund seeded by Sequoia partners; co-led Wave's landmark $200M round, Africa's largest Series A at the time.

HSBC.about: Europe's largest bank; made a strategic investment in Monese as part of its digital-banking partnerships push.

Hummingbird Ventures.about: Antwerp-based early-stage firm hunting outlier founders in overlooked geographies; early Kraken investor, now behind its Krak app.

Icehouse Ventures.about: Auckland's most active early-stage investor, spun out of The Icehouse incubator; backed Kiwi tax-and-banking app Hnry from the start.

IIFL.about: Mumbai-listed financial services group (IIFL Finance/Securities); its funds invested in SMB-neobank Open.

Insignia Ventures.about: Singapore fund founded 2017 by ex-Sequoia partner Yinglan Tan; Southeast Asia specialist behind multi-currency app YouTrip.

Intesa Sanpaolo.about: Italy's largest bank; strategic anchor of Swiss digital private bank Alpian while running its own digital spin-off, Isybank.

Janngo Capital.about: Pan-African fund founded by Jumia veteran Fatoumata Bâ, deploying from Abidjan and Paris with a gender-lens mandate; backed Ivory Coast's Djamo.

JPMorgan Chase.about: America's biggest bank bought ~40% of Brazil's C6 Bank in 2021 and has since increased the stake — the clearest big-bank endorsement of the neobank model.

Jump Crypto.about: The crypto division of Chicago trading giant Jump Trading; infrastructure-grade backer of Solana ecosystem apps including Phantom.

KakaoBank.about: Korea's dominant digital bank invested in Indonesia's Superbank — a rare case of one neobank writing strategic cheques into another.

Khosla Ventures.about: Sun Microsystems co-founder Vinod Khosla's firm, founded 2004; famously early into Stripe, Square and Ramp.

KKR.about: The New York buyout pioneer founded 1976; growth-equity position in the Philippines' Maya.

Kleiner Perkins.about: The 1972 Sand Hill Road icon (Genentech, Google, Amazon); joined Toss Bank's parent Viva Republica cap table.

Krealo (Credicorp).about: The corporate venture arm of Credicorp, Peru's largest financial group; created and backs Chile's Tenpo.

Launch Africa.about: Pan-African seed fund and one of the continent's most active investors (140+ startups across 22 countries); backed Tunisia's Flouci.

Left Lane Capital.about: New York growth fund focused on consumer internet; international portfolio including New Zealand's Hnry.

LocalGlobe.about: The Klein family's London seed firm (now part of Phoenix Court); seeded Tide, Wise, Monzo and much of UK fintech.

Lone Pine Capital.about: Greenwich 'Tiger cub' hedge fund founded 1997; crossover growth position in US challenger Varo.

M13.about: Los Angeles consumer-focused VC founded by the Reum brothers; backed business-banking platform Rho.

Magma Partners.about: Santiago-born, LatAm-wide early-stage firm; first-money specialist in Latin fintech, including Chile's Global66.

Meritech Capital.about: Palo Alto late-stage firm (Salesforce, Facebook, Snowflake pre-IPO); joined Wealthsimple's growth rounds.

Microsoft (M12).about: Microsoft's venture fund; strategic investor in ConsenSys, the company behind MetaMask.

Mountain Nazca.about: Mexico City venture firm; early institutional backer of albo, one of Mexico's first challenger banks.

Mouro Capital.about: Fintech-only fund anchored by Santander (successor to Santander InnoVentures); led rounds in Mexico's Klar.

Mubadala.about: Abu Dhabi's $300B+ sovereign investor; Wio Bank consortium member and prolific global fintech LP/direct investor.

Musha Ventures.about: Early-stage fund backing African startups at pre-seed and seed; among Flouci's first institutional investors.

NEA.about: New Enterprise Associates, founded 1977 — one of the largest venture firms in the world; backed spend-management neobank Slash.

NetEase.about: The Chinese internet and gaming company; early corporate backer of Transsion-affiliated PalmPay in Nigeria.

Norrsken22.about: African growth fund raised with backing from the Norrsken network of Klarna co-founder Niklas Adalberth and 30+ unicorn founders; TymeBank growth investor.

Norwest Venture Partners.about: Palo Alto multi-stage firm managing Wells Fargo capital since 1961; growth position in cash-advance pioneer Dave.

Nubank.about: The world's largest independent neobank became an investor itself — a $150M strategic stake in South Africa's TymeBank in 2024.

OKX Ventures.about: The investment arm of crypto exchange OKX; backs on-chain consumer finance including EtherFi's cash card.

Oui Capital.about: Lagos- and Boston-based pan-African early-stage fund founded 2019; its ~$150K first cheque into Moniepoint (then TeamApt) famously returned the entire debut fund many times over.

Owl Ventures.about: The world's largest edtech-focused VC (San Francisco); backed Greenlight where family banking meets financial education.

Paradigm.about: The crypto mega-fund founded 2018 by Coinbase co-founder Fred Ehrsam and Sequoia alum Matt Huang; led Phantom's growth rounds.

ParaFi Capital.about: DeFi-specialist investment firm founded by KKR alum Ben Forman; institutional backer of the MetaMask ecosystem via ConsenSys.

Passion Capital.about: London pre-seed firm; wrote Monzo's very first cheque and incubated much of UK fintech's founding generation.

Permira.about: London-headquartered private-equity firm (~€80B); took a significant Klarna stake in its 2017 round.

PLDT.about: The Philippine telecom group; Maya (formerly PayMaya/Voyager) grew out of PLDT's digital unit and remains its fintech flagship.

Polychain Capital.about: Founded 2016 by Coinbase employee #1 Olaf Carlson-Wee; the first crypto fund to cross $1B, backer of hybrid crypto-banking app Juno.

Portage.about: Montreal-based fintech-only investor within Sagard (Power Corporation's alternatives arm); long-time KOHO lead.

Power Corporation.about: The Desmarais family's Canadian holding company; Wealthsimple's controlling shareholder through IGM Financial.

Prime Venture Partners.about: Bangalore early-stage fund with a fintech-first thesis; seeded banking-app Niyo.

Recharge Capital.about: New York thematic fund deploying into fintech and healthcare in emerging markets; backed Pakistan's SadaPay.

REYL Intesa Sanpaolo.about: Geneva private bank REYL (majority-owned by Intesa Sanpaolo's Fideuram); founding backer of Swiss digital private bank Alpian.

Samsung Next.about: Samsung's venture and innovation arm; backed MPC-wallet pioneer Zengo.

SBI Group.about: Tokyo-listed financial conglomerate spun out of SoftBank in 2000; strategic investor in Nigeria's Kuda among global fintech positions.

Section 32.about: Founded by Google Ventures creator Bill Maris; frontier tech and health focus with fintech positions including Dave.

Seed Capital.about: Copenhagen's leading seed fund; first institutional money into Danish neobank Lunar.

Seven Seven Six.about: Reddit co-founder Alexis Ohanian's venture firm; backed Ethereum wallet Rainbow.

SignalFire.about: San Francisco firm built around a data platform for sourcing and supporting startups; early backer of creator-banking startup Karat.

Singtel.about: Southeast Asia's telecom heavyweight; co-anchor of Indonesia's Superbank with Grab, Emtek and KakaoBank.

Singular.about: Paris firm founded by ex-Alven partners; backed freelancer-insurance-and-banking app Indy.

Slow Ventures.about: San Francisco generalist seed fund founded by early Facebook alumni; backed stablecoin-payments app Sling Money.

Soros Fund Management.about: George Soros' family office; growth investor in Argentina's Ualá alongside SoftBank and Tencent.

Source Code Capital.about: Beijing venture firm founded by ex-Sequoia China partner Cao Yi (ByteDance, Meituan); backed OPay's early expansion.

South Park Commons.about: San Francisco community-fund hybrid founded by Facebook's first female engineer Ruchi Sanghvi; backs founders at the idea stage, including Comun's.

Ten31.about: Bitcoin-only investment firm; the largest dedicated backer of bitcoin companies including Strike's parent Zap.

The Rise Fund (TPG).about: TPG's $10B+ impact-investing platform co-founded with Bono and Jeff Skoll; anchor investor in mission-driven US challenger Varo.

Transsion.about: The Shenzhen phone maker whose Tecno and Infinix brands dominate Africa; PalmPay ships pre-installed on its devices — distribution as capital.

Tribe Capital.about: San Francisco fund using quantitative product-market-fit analysis (ex-Social Capital team); backer of Kraken's consumer app Krak.

Truist.about: The US super-regional bank formed by the BB&T–SunTrust merger; strategic investor in Atlanta-based Greenwood.

TTV Capital.about: Atlanta fund investing exclusively in fintech since 2000; early Greenlight believer in its hometown.

Variant.about: Crypto fund founded by ex-a16z partner Jesse Walden around the 'ownership economy' thesis; early Phantom backer.

Visa.about: The card network invests strategically where new rails meet its own — a $200M stake in Moniepoint made it the Nigerian unicorn's marquee backer.

Warwick Capital Partners.about: London special-situations and credit manager; a cornerstone institutional shareholder in UK SMB lender Allica Bank.

Z47 (Matrix India).about: Matrix Partners India, rebranded Z47 in 2024; founded by Baazee (eBay India) creator Avnish Bajaj, with early positions in Indian fintech including Jupiter.
